original_hypothesis.title: Missing Authorization in updateOrder Allows Any User to Modify Any Active Order
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The updateOrder function in MarginTradingHook does not verify that the order being modified belongs to the caller's position. Unlike cancelOrder which checks `order.initPosId == initPosId`, updateOrder only verifies the caller has any valid position and sufficient collateral. An attacker can modify any victim's active order parameters (triggerPrice, limitPrice, tokenOut, collAmt) and then fill the modified order to steal the victim's collateral.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Direct comparison of cancelOrder (has auth check at line ~248) vs updateOrder (missing auth check) reveals the omission. The fillOrder function trusts stored order parameters, making this a complete attack chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cancelOrder has `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)` at line ~248 but updateOrder at line ~330 omits this check entirely while modifying order storage

preconditions: Victim has at least one active stop-loss or take-profit order
Attacker has their own valid margin position with non-zero collateral
Attacker knows the victim's orderId (observable on-chain)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing ownership check: order.initPosId == initPosId not verified
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: updateOrder reads initPosId from caller's mapping but never asserts the order's initPosId matches; order fields are directly overwritten in storage

analysis_questions: Can the attacker set tokenOut to a valueless ERC20 to minimize the cost of filling?
Does changing limitPrice_e36 allow extracting maximum collateral value?
Can triggerPrice be set to ensure immediate fillability?
validation_requirements: Construct PoC: create attacker position, call updateOrder with victim's orderId, modify limitPrice to extreme value, then call fillOrder
Verify collateral extraction exceeds cost of repaying debt
Confirm no other check prevents cross-position order modification

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Attacker creates their own margin position via openPos. 2) Attacker calls updateOrder(attackerPosId, victimOrderId, ...) setting limitPrice to extreme value favoring the attacker. 3) Attacker calls fillOrder(victimOrderId), paying minimal borrToken repayment while receiving victim's collateral shares worth significantly more.
prerequisites: Attacker has a valid position with non-zero collateral

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete theft of collateral from any user with active margin orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with active stop-loss or take-profit orders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery — collateral transferred to attacker via decollateralize

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing updateOrder and cancelOrder authorization paths

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Attacker needs enough tokens to repay proportional debt
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Attack visible on-chain via UpdateOrder event with mismatched initPosId

validation_result.mechanism: Missing order ownership check in updateOrder
validation_result.impact_summary: Any user with a valid margin position can modify any other user's active stop-loss/take-profit order parameters (triggerPrice, limitPrice, collAmt, tokenOut), then fill the modified order to steal the victim's collateral.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `updateOrder` function at MarginTradingHook.sol lines 330-354 retrieves `initPosId` from the caller's own mapping (`initPosIds[msg.sender][_posId]`) and verifies it is non-zero, but never asserts that `order.initPosId == initPosId`. This means the caller's position ID is used only for the collateral feasibility check, while the order being modified can belong to any other user's position. In contrast, `cancelOrder` at lines 239-248 explicitly includes the check `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)`. The omission in `updateOrder` creates a complete attack chain: an attacker modifies a victim's order's `limitPrice_e36` and `triggerPrice_e36` to extreme values, then calls `fillOrder` which trusts the stored order parameters and decollateralizes the victim's collateral to the executor (attacker).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct comparison of `cancelOrder` and `updateOrder` reveals the exact missing line. cancelOrder line ~244: `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT);`. updateOrder has no equivalent check. The order storage is directly modified at lines 348-351 without any ownership verification linking the orderId to the caller's position.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)` check. The function validates the caller has *some* position but never verifies the order belongs to that position. All four mutable order fields (triggerPrice, limitPrice, collAmt, tokenOut) are overwritten in storage for any active order.

This function correctly includes `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT)` at line ~244, proving the developer intended this authorization pattern but accidentally omitted it in updateOrder.

fillOrder trusts stored order parameters (modified by attacker via updateOrder). It uses order.initPosId (victim's position) for decollateralize but uses attacker-modified limitPrice_e36 for amtOut calculation, enabling extraction of victim's collateral at manipulated prices.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls updateOrder(attackerPosId, victimOrderId, extremeTriggerPrice, cheapTokenOut, extremeLimitPrice, collAmt)
2. initPosId = initPosIds[attacker][attackerPosId] → attacker's initPosId (non-zero, passes check)
3. marginPos = __marginPositions[attackerInitPosId] → attacker's margin position (used for collAmt check only)
4. collAmt check passes against attacker's position collateral
5. order storage for victimOrderId is directly overwritten: triggerPrice, limitPrice, collAmt, tokenOut
7. order.initPosId still points to victim's position, marginPos loaded from victim's data
8. Modified triggerPrice passes validation against oracle
10. Attacker pays minimal amtOut + repayAmt, receives victim's collateral via decollateralize
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: updateOrder reads `initPosId = initPosIds[msg.sender][_posId]` at line ~342 and checks `initPosId != 0` at line ~343, but never checks `order.initPosId == initPosId`. Lines 348-351 directly modify `__orders[_orderId]` storage fields. In fillOrder (line ~251), the order is loaded with `order.initPosId` (victim's unchanged value) but with attacker-modified `limitPrice_e36`, `triggerPrice_e36`, `collAmt`, and `tokenOut`. At line ~284, `IInitCore(CORE).decollateralize(order.initPosId, marginPos.collPool, order.collAmt, msg.sender)` sends victim's collateral to the attacker (msg.sender/executor).

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability is in application logic, not configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully viable: (1) Attacker creates a margin position via openPos with minimal collateral. (2) Attacker observes victim's orderId on-chain (orderIds are sequential from lastOrderId, events are emitted). (3) Attacker calls updateOrder(attackerPosId, victimOrderId, ...) setting triggerPrice to be immediately satisfiable and limitPrice to extreme value minimizing amtOut. (4) Attacker calls fillOrder(victimOrderId) as executor, paying minimal borrToken repayment and minimal tokenOut, receiving victim's collateral shares. The MarginTradingHook owns all positions' NFTs (created via openPos→createPos), so it is authorized for both repay and decollateralize on any position it manages.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Any user with active stop-loss or take-profit orders can have their collateral stolen. The attacker can systematically target all active orders. The collateral is transferred via decollateralize to the attacker with no recovery mechanism. The only cost to the attacker is: (a) creating a minimal position, (b) paying the proportional debt repayment (which is offset by the collateral value received), and (c) paying a small amtOut in tokenOut to the victim.
recommended_mitigations: Add `_require(order.initPosId == initPosId, Errors.INVALID_INPUT);` to updateOrder, matching the pattern in cancelOrder
Consider adding a comprehensive test that verifies cross-position order modification is impossible for all order mutation functions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system (InitCore, PosManager, LendingPool, MarginTradingHook, oracle, config). 2. User A opens margin position and creates a stop-loss order with orderId X. 3. User B opens their own margin position. 4. User B calls updateOrder(B_posId, X, newTriggerPrice, newTokenOut, extremeLimitPrice, collAmt) - should succeed. 5. Verify __orders[X] storage was modified. 6. User B calls fillOrder(X) providing minimal tokens. 7. Verify User A's collateral was transferred to User B.
uncertainties: The exact economic profit depends on the specific limitPrice manipulation and the debt-to-collateral ratio of the victim's position
The _calculateRepaySize return value swap bug (separate issue) may cause fillOrder to revert in some cases, partially mitigating exploitation in pools with diverged share ratios
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test: verify no caller can modify an order where order.initPosId != caller's initPosId
Integration test: full attack scenario with actual token transfers to quantify profit
Verify whether the _calculateRepaySize return value swap affects exploitability
